[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]]'''Ōtomo no Tabito''' (大伴旅人; c. [[662]]-[[731]] ) was a [[Japan]]ese poet of an old time, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to compiling the [[Man'yōshū]] alongside the father.
{{Japan-writer-stub}}
[[Category:Japanese poets]] [[Category:662 births]] [[Category:731 deaths]]
[[de:Ōtomo no Tabito]] [[ja:大伴旅人]]

[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]]'''Ōtomo no Tabito''' (大伴旅人; c. [[662]]-[[731]] ) was a [[Japan]]ese poet of an old time, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to compiling the [[Man'yōshū]] alongside the father. Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governer-General of Dazaifu, the military procuracy in northern Kyūshū from 728-730 ACE, Tabito hosted a plum-blossom party, encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordiantes in imatation of Chinese style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen [[tanka]] in praise of sake.
{{Japan-writer-stub}}
[[Category:Japanese poets]] [[Category:662 births]] [[Category:731 deaths]]
[[de:Ōtomo no Tabito]] [[ja:大伴旅人]]

[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]]'''Ōtomo no Tabito''' (大伴旅人; c. [[662]]-[[731]] ) was a [[Japan]]ese poet of an old time, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to compiling the [[Man'yōshū]] alongside the father. Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governer-General of Dazaifu, the military procuracy in northern [[Kyūshū]] from 728-730 ACE, Tabito hosted a plum-blossom party, encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordiantes in imatation of Chinese style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen [[tanka]] in praise of sake.

[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]]
'''Ōtomo no Tabito''' (大伴旅人; c. [[662]]-[[731]]) was a [[Japan]]ese poet, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to compiling the [[Man'yōshū]] alongside his father. Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governer-General of [[Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern [[Kyūshū]] from 728-730, Tabito hosted a plum-blossom party, encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of Chinese style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]].

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=May 2007}}
[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]] {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - August 31, 731}} was a Japanese military leader and poet, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father.
== Life == Ōtomo was born in 665.
During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, Hozumi no Oyu, in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}}</ref>
In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Kyoto]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area.<ref name=":0" /> As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]].
In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]].
Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, [[Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|Ōtomo no Iratsume]], as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment was a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}}</ref>
Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]].
{{Authority control}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} [[Category:Japanese poets]] [[Category:662 births]] [[Category:731 deaths]] [[Category:Place of birth unknown]] [[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[Category:Place of death unknown]] [[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:Man'yō poets]]
{{Japan-writer-stub}}

[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]] {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - August 31, 731}} was a Japanese military leader and poet, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father.
== Life == Ōtomo was born in 665.
During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, Hozumi no Oyu, in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}}</ref>
In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Kyoto]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area.<ref name=":0" /> As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]].
In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]].
Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, [[Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|Ōtomo no Iratsume]], as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment was a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}}</ref>
Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]].
{{Authority control}}

{{Japan-writer-stub}}

[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]] {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - August 31, 731}} was a Japanese military leader and poet, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father.
== Life == Ōtomo was born in 665.
During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, Hozumi no Oyu, in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}}</ref>
In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Kyoto]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area.<ref name=":0" /> As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]].
In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]].
Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, [[Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|Ōtomo no Iratsume]], as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment was a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}}</ref>
Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]].

revision.text:
[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]] {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - August 31, 731}} was a Japanese military leader and poet, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father.
== Life == Ōtomo was born in 665.
During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, Hozumi no Oyu, in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}}</ref>
In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Kyoto]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area.<ref name=":0" /> As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]].
In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]].
Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, [[Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|Ōtomo no Iratsume]], as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}}</ref> However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}}</ref>
Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]].

[[Image:Otomo Tabito.jpg|thumb|300px|Ōtomo no Tabito]] {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - August 31, 731}} was a Japanese military leader and poet, best known as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father.
== Life == Ōtomo was born in 665.
During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, Hozumi no Oyu, in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}}</ref>
In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Kyoto]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area.<ref name=":0" /> As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]].
In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]].
Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, [[Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|Ōtomo no Iratsume]], as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawakobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}}</ref> However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}}</ref> In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}}</ref>
Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]].

== Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]].
During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}}</ref>
In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Kyoto]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}}</ref>
In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade.
In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Kyoto]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area.<ref name=":0" /> As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]].
In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]].
Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}}</ref> However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}}</ref> In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}}</ref>
In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}}</ref>
Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]].

After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} [[Category:Japanese poets]] [[Category:662 births]] [[Category:731 deaths]] [[Category:Place of birth unknown]] [[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[Category:Place of death unknown]] [[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:Man'yō poets]] {{Japan-writer-stub}}

revision.comment: /* Life */ This was a Nara period courtier, so when he was summoned back to the capital, that capital was Nara and not Kyoto. Kyoto hadn't been founded yet.

In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} [[Category:Japanese poets]] [[Category:662 births]] [[Category:731 deaths]] [[Category:Place of birth unknown]] [[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[Category:Place of death unknown]] [[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:Man'yō poets]] {{Japan-writer-stub}}

In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Japanese poets]] [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:660s births]] [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:731 deaths]] [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Place of birth unknown]] [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Place of death unknown]] [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:uncategorized pages]][[Category:Man'yō poets]] [[Category:dd]] {{Japan-writer-stub}}

{{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} 大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} 大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} 大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}} 大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. {{Short description|Japanese poet}} {{family name hatnote|Ōtomo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Ōtomo no Tabito
大伴旅人 | image = Otomo Tabito.jpg | caption = Ōtomo no Tabito in ''[[Zenken Kojitsu]]'' by [[Kikuchi Yōsai]] | birth_date = 665 | death_date = 31 August 731 | nationality = [[Japanese people|Japanese]] | occupation = [[Kugyō|court noble]], poet | mother = Iratsume | father = [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]] | spouse = Ōtomo no Iratsume }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōtomo no Tabito'''|大伴 旅人||665 - 31 August 731}} was a Japanese [[Kugyō|court noble]], military leader and poet. He is known for his military campaign against the [[Hayato rebellion|Hayato Rebellion]] and as the father of [[Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], who contributed to the compilation of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' alongside his father. He served as [[Dainagon]] and held the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Second Rank]]. == Life == Ōtomo was born in 665, the son of [[Dainagon]] [[Ōtomo no Yasumaro]]. His mother was Iratsume, the daughter of [[Kose no Hito]]. During [[Empress Genmei]]'s New Year's Day celebration of 710, he led the cavalry, [[Hayato people|Hayato]], [[Emishi]], and others as the [[Shogun]] of the Left with his deputy, [[Hozumi no Oyu]], in a march along the [[Suzaku Avenue]].{{Cite book |last=Ujitani |first=Tsutomu |title=続日本紀 （上） |publisher=Kodansha |year=1992 |at=和銅3年正月1日条, 養老4年6月17日条, 養老4年8月12日条}} In 711, he was given the [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court rank]] of [[Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade]], and in 715, he was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, [[Ministry of the Center|Minister of the Center]]. In 718, he was promoted to [[Chūnagon]], and in 719, he was promoted Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. In 720, when the [[Hayato Rebellion]] erupted in [[Kyushu]] after the assassination of Governor of [[Ōsumi Province]] Yako no Maro, Ōtomo was ordered to lead Japanese forces to suppress the rebellion. After the death of [[Minister of the Right]] [[Fujiwara no Fuhito]], Ōtomo received an imperial edict to return to [[Heijō-kyō|Nara]]. However, since the Hayato had not yet been pacified, the deputy shogun and his men were ordered to continue garrisoning the area. As a result, southern Kyushu was firmly brought under the control of the Imperial court under [[Empress Genmei]]. In 721, he was promoted to Junior Third Rank, and in 724, he was promoted to Senior Third Rank upon the accession to the throne of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Around 728, Ōtomo was transferred to [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] with his wife, Ōtomo no Iratsume, as Governor of Dazaifu, the second time he was sent to Kyushu after his 60th birthday, and this appointment could have been a strategic demotion by the four Fujiwara brothers, [[Fujiwara no Muchimaro|Muchimaro]], [[Fujiwara no Fusasaki|Fusasaki]], [[Fujiwara no Umakai|Umakai]] and [[Fujiwara no Maro|Maro]], to oust [[Prince Nagaya]], the [[Minister of the Left]] in power at the time.{{Cite book |last=Nomura |first=Tadao |title=律令政治の諸様相 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |year=1968 |at=神亀5年 大伴旅人}} However, it has also been theorized that Ōtomo was transferred because of his excellent skills in diplomacy and defense in light of the international situation at the time.{{Cite book |last=Masuo |first=Shin'ichirō |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/37634692 |title=Manyō kajin to Chūgoku shisō |last2=増尾伸一郎 |date=1997 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=4-642-02310-0 |at=大伴旅人 |oclc=37634692}} In Dazaifu, he compiled the ''Chikushi Kadan'' of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' with [[Yamanoue no Okura]] and [[Sami Mansei]]. After arriving in Dazaifu, his wife died.{{Cite book |title=Man'yōshū |year= |at=巻5-795}} In 730, he was appointed Dainagon.{{Cite book |last=Kuroita |first=Katsumi |title=新訂増補国史大系 公卿補任 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan |others=国史大系編修会 |at=大伴旅人}} Tabito was a contemporary of [[Hitomaro]], but lacked his success in the Imperial Court. While serving as Governor-General of [[Dazaifu, Fukuoka|Dazaifu]], the military procuracy in northern Kyushu from 728-730, Tabito hosted a [[Hanami|plum-blossom party]], encouraging the composition of poetry among his subordinates in imitation of [[Chinese poetry|Chinese]] style elegance. He also showed his Chinese education in his set of thirteen ''[[waka (poetry)|tanka]]'' in praise of [[sake]]. == References == {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Otomo no, Tabito}}
